answer:
### Manusmṛti (Manu)

Manu provides one of the most comprehensive treatments of witnesses in Chapter 8, emphasizing both procedural qualifications/exclusions and a profound spiritual dimension. Admissible testimony requires witnesses who are **householders of good lineage from the twice-born varṇas or virtuous Shudras** (Kṣatriya, Vaiśya, Śūdra), **trustworthy, Vedic-knowers, non-greedy**, and free from faults like kinship, enmity, disease, or impairment.[^1] Incompetents include relatives, enemies, kings, artisans, actors, ascetics, dependents, robbers, intoxicated/insane/hungry/weary/lustful/angry persons, and the impaired.[^2] Women testify for women (and similarly by varṇa), but in exigencies (house/forest/life-death cases), anyone who saw/heard may testify; otherwise, even women/children/kinsmen/slaves.[^3] In conflicts, prioritize majority, then quality (best twice-born).[^4]

The **procedure** involves the judge questioning witnesses in court (plaintiff/defendant present), facing north/east, forenoon, with a consoling formula: "Speak truthfully what you know of their conduct."[^5] Varna-specific oaths invoke truth/cows/grain/sins.[^6] Testimony must match exactly what was seen/heard; contradictions (country/time/measure) invalidate.[^7]

**Spiritual emphasis** dominates: Truth purifies; the Self is the ultimate witness/refuge; gods/heaven/earth/water/heart/moon/sun/fire/Yama/wind/Dharma observe all; false testimony slays kinsmen (5 for cattle, 1000 for man), sends to hell (fetters of Varuṇa), or beggar-life; merits lost to dogs; permitted lies only to save life.[^8] Witnesses gain worlds/fame for truth.[^9]

### Yājñavalkya Smṛti

Yājñavalkya (Chapter 2) focuses on **practical qualifications**: witnesses should be **ascetics/charitable/good family/truthful/dharmic/upright/with sons/wealth**, at least three (same varṇa/caste preferred, or universal).[^10] Incompetents: women/children/aged/gamblers/intoxicated/insane/sin-accused/actors/heretics/forgers/defective senses/outcastes/friends/interested parties/enemies/thieves/violent/convicted/banished.[^11] One dharmic witness suffices if approved by both; anyone in adultery/theft/assault/violence.[^12]

**Procedure**: Admonish with hell-world threats/merits lost (slayer/Brahmin-killer worlds; past merits ruined).[^13] Non-appearance: pay full debt +10% fine (46th day).[^14] Conflicts: majority > virtue > most virtuous.[^15] New/better witnesses override prior.[^16] False witnesses: double dispute fine (Brahmin banished).[^17] Exception: lie to avert death (expiate via Sarasvatī oblation).[^18]

Balanced procedural/spiritual, but leans procedural (lists, conflicts, punishments).

### Bṛhaspati Smṛti

Bṛhaspati (Chapter 1, §1.5) is intensely **procedural**, cataloging **12 witness types** (subscribed/recorded/concealed/reminded/kinsman/messenger/chance/secondary/present/king/chief judge/village), with 9/7/5/4/3/2 Brahmins ideal; singles for gamblers/stake-seizers/king/judge.[^19] Incompetents: thieves/violent/eunuchs/gamblers/informers.[^20]

**Procedure** dominates: Impeach faults timely (pre-proof); establish via defendant; if fail, defendant fined; if succeed, witnesses barred.[^21] Admonish: merits ruined/false=sin.[^22] Conflicts: number > qualities > religious acts > honesty.[^23] Exact conformity required; contradictions (country/time/age/object/measure/appearance/caste) invalidate; deficiency/excess rejects testimony (no punishment unless silent).[^24] Part proven=whole in sex/violence/theft.[^25]

Minimal spiritual (merits lost); exhaustive process (types/faults/impeachment/conflicts).

**Judgment**: **Bṛhaspati's is most procedural** (12 types, impeachment rituals, granular contradictions/conflicts). **Manu's is most spiritual** (cosmic/internal witnesses, vivid afterlife/karmic penalties, Self/Dharma as core). Yājñavalkya mediates.

[^1]: *Manusmṛti*, Chapter 8, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/08/translation/#p-803bc3).

[^10]: *Yājñavalkya Smṛti*, Chapter 2, [paragraph](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/02/translation/#p-5c0bb3) (Yj_2.68-69).
[^11]: *Yājñavalkya Smṛti*, Chapter 2, [paragraphs](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/02/translation/#p-478abd) (Yj_2.70-71).
